title: Photographs: a single image vs. an album
intro: Photographs raise the same fork as most material: are you describing one image, or a whole album? This scenario branches on that, then separates the picture (intellectual content) from the print or negative (physical carrier), and attaches the photographer — the three moves that keep a photo collection queryable.
question: Is a photograph a Record, and the album a Record Set? And where do the print, the negative, and the photographer go?

steps.prompt: What are you cataloguing — one photograph, or a whole album/collection?

choices.label: A single photograph
choices.why: Good — one image is one unit of content: a Record.

choices.label: An album / collection
choices.why: Good — an album is an aggregation of photographs that belong together: a Record Set, with each photo a member.

choices.label: An Instantiation
choices.why: Doesn't fit as the starting point. An Instantiation is the physical print or negative — a carrier of a photograph's content, which you'll add after choosing the intellectual unit (Record or Record Set).

steps.prompt: Model the single photograph (the image itself).

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Fits. The photograph's content is a Record. Its print and negative are Instantiations of it; the people or places depicted attach as subjects.

choices.label: Instantiation
choices.why: That's the physical print/negative, not the image's content. Keep the image as a Record and add the carrier as an Instantiation.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Too much for one image — a Set is for the album.

capture.comment: Create the photograph as a Record.

body.title: Portrait of the harbour master, c.1910

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Fits. The album is an aggregation of photographs — a Record Set. Each photograph is a Record that is part of it.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Undersells it — one Record can't hold many distinct images with their own captions, dates and photographers. Use a Record Set of photo Records.

choices.label: Record Part
choices.why: Doesn't fit. The album isn't a component of a larger record; it's an aggregation. (A photo's caption slip could be a Record Part of that photo.)

capture.comment: Create the album as a Record Set.

choices.label: Agent (Person) + has_creator
choices.why: Fits. The photographer is a Person (an Agent). Create them once and relate the photograph/album to them with has_creator; reuse the same Agent across everything they shot.

choices.label: Put the name in the caption text
choices.why: Doesn't fit. A name in free text can't be reconciled or linked to the photographer's other work. Model them as a first-class Agent.

capture.comment: Create the photographer as an Agent.
Link the photograph/album to the photographer with has_creator.

steps.prompt: Finally — the physical print and the glass-plate negative. Where do they go?

choices.label: Instantiation(s)
choices.why: Fits. Each physical carrier — the print, the negative, a later scan — is an Instantiation of the photograph Record, linked with has_instantiation. That's how one image can have many carriers, each with its own format and condition.

choices.label: Separate Records
choices.why: Doesn't fit. The print and negative aren't different photographs — they're different carriers of the same image. Model them as Instantiations of the one Record.

choices.label: Attributes on the photograph
choices.why: Too flat. Carriers have their own format, dimensions and condition and there can be several — they deserve to be Instantiation entities.

capture.comment: Create the physical carrier as an Instantiation.
Link the photograph to its carrier with has_instantiation. Repeat for the negative and any scans.

outcome.verdict: Image, carriers and creator — cleanly separated.
outcome.summary: The image is a Record (or the album a Record Set); the print and negative are Instantiations of it; the photographer is a reusable Agent. Now you can ask 'every photo by this photographer' or 'all carriers of this image' — and add the people and places depicted as subjects whenever you're ready.

links.label: See real RiC data in the graph viewer
